title: The Disproportionate-Assets Case and the Kodanad Case!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source pages restore two English Supreme Court quotations truncated by OCR, including `It is bereft of bonafide, we will not entertain such matters` and the complete “Bench hunting” passage. They are retained as printed. Source comparison also confirms repairs to `பார்வையிடாமல்`, `சொத்துக்களை`, `குற்றம் சாட்டப்பட்டோர்`, `வேண்டுமென்று`, `உச்சநீதிமன்றத்தில்`, `மேல்முறையீடு`, `பார்த்து`, `கோருவதற்கு`, and the closing `எதிர்பார்க்கிறது`. These corrections are confined to the derived Tamil reading copy. The printed salutation is restored; canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: Yesterday, 12 December 2013, the Special Judge ordered that the movable properties in Chennai connected with the disproportionate-assets case against Chief Minister Jayalalithaa be brought to the Special Court in Bengaluru, where the trial is under way, by the 21st.
In my “Udanpirappu” letter of 14 September 2013 about the disproportionate-assets case, I wrote:

> The seized jewellery constituting material evidence in this important case is held in the Reserve Bank vault in Chennai. During the trial, all the jewellery already seized must be brought under the Special Court's custody. That has not yet been done. Before final arguments, the Special Court issued no order for the jewellery to be produced before it. The judge appears anxious to deliver judgment before the jewellery is produced in the Special Court and examined, perhaps because he wishes to do so before retiring. Under the law, judgment cannot be delivered without bringing the jewellery to the Bengaluru court and inspecting it.
Accordingly, party Member of Parliament and advocate, younger brother R. Thamaraiselvan, filed a petition in the Bengaluru Special Court on behalf of Professor. The petition said:

> When the Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption searched the homes of Jayalalithaa and Sasikala in 1997, it seized 1,066 movable properties, including gold, diamond and silver jewellery, silk saris and expensive watches. Those properties are held at the Reserve Bank and government treasury in Chennai. Although the trial is nearing completion, none of the movable articles in Chennai has been brought to the Bengaluru Special Court. Only a list describing the articles has been filed. It would not be proper to deliver judgment on that basis. The movable properties in Chennai must therefore be brought to Bengaluru.

When the matter came before Judge John Michael D'Cunha on 6 December 2013, advocate Thamaraiselvan argued that under criminal-procedure law, the jewellery should be brought to the Bengaluru court. Jayalalithaa's counsel, P. Kumar, argued that there was no need to bring the movable properties connected with the case from Chennai.
Party advocate P. Kumaresan, arguing for General Secretary Professor, said:

> We have not filed this petition out of political animosity. Because the public prosecutor failed to perform his duty properly, we filed it to ensure that all jewellery connected with the trial remains under the control of this court, where the case is being heard. Under Section 452 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, this court must deliver judgment only after obtaining custody of the articles connected with the case.
When the matter came up on the 12th, advocates P. Kumaresan, Thamaraiselvan and Saravanan appeared for the party. Judge John Michael D'Cunha stated in his ruling:

> The court cannot accept Jayalalithaa's contention that the petitioner has no authority to ask this court to bring the 1,066 movable properties, including the gold, silver and diamond jewellery seized from the accused in this disproportionate-assets case, to the Bengaluru Special Court. The Supreme Court and the Karnataka High Court have already recognised the petitioner's right in this case. It is established that the petitioner has an interest in the matter. > > The petitioner does not ask to remove the public prosecutor and conduct the case himself. Rather, because the public prosecutor failed to draw attention to something he should have identified, the petitioner has brought it to this court's notice through this petition. It would not be proper to view this as having some other motive, and it must not be viewed that way. This Special Court has already permitted the petitioner to assist the public prosecutor in conducting the case. On that basis, the court considers that the petitioner filed this petition on the public prosecutor's behalf. The petitioner's, that is Professor's, petition is therefore allowed.
The judge further explained:

> The articles connected with a case must be held by the court in which that case is tried. The case was transferred to this court while the Chennai court had received and retained control of the articles and was conducting the trial. There is now no evidence that the Chennai court transferred those articles when it transferred the case. The lawful procedure is for the court conducting the case to retain control of its properties. The properties connected with this case must therefore be transferred from the Chennai court to this court. Television channels and newspapers must avoid publishing reports about these articles.

Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh too submitted a petition to the judge stating that “the request of the DMK advocates is reasonable.” This is the same Bhavani Singh who had until then acted in support of Jayalalithaa's side. When the DMK sought his removal, the accused litigated all the way to the Supreme Court to insist that this very advocate remain public prosecutor, meaning that he must appear against Jayalalithaa's side. That too is worth noting.

Along with the unusual request that Bhavani Singh alone continue as public prosecutor, Jayalalithaa placed another strange request before the Supreme Court. She wanted Judge Balakrishna, who had been trying the case, to continue hearing it even after retirement. But Judge Balakrishna himself did not wish to continue. Judge John Michael D'Cunha is therefore now hearing the case and has delivered this ruling.

Everyone was astonished that persons accused in a case should petition the Supreme Court to demand that one particular advocate alone prosecute them and one particular judge alone continue hearing and deciding their case even after retirement.
When Jayalalithaa's side went as far as the Supreme Court demanding that Judge Balakrishna continue to conduct the case, something came to my mind.

On 4 April 2008, *The Hindu* reported a Supreme Court judgment concerning the Kodanad Estate. Members of the public complained to the Revenue Divisional Officer, the Sub-Divisional Magistrate, that the estate manager had erected a gate across a road long used by the public in the Nilgiris district and prevented anyone from passing. They asked that it be removed. The officer ordered the gate removed within twenty-four hours, warning that failure would invite action under the Indian Penal Code.

The Kodanad Estate manager challenged that order in the Madras High Court. The case was first listed before one judge, who was unable to take up that particular matter, and was then transferred to another.

Jayalalithaa and Sasikala's side objected to the transfer before the Chief Justice of the High Court. The Chief Justice ruled that assigning a judge lay within the Chief Justice's exclusive authority. Did they stop there?

The Kodanad Estate manager appealed against that order to the Supreme Court. The case came before the Chief Justice of India on 4 April 2008. The Chief Justice said that the manager's request was without justification and that such petitions could not be entertained:
> When Counsel said that it was a bonafide petition, the Chief Justice of India said, “It is bereft of bonafide, we will not entertain such matters”.
The Chief Justice asked the Kodanad Estate's counsel:

> What do you want? You are Bench hunting. The Chief Justice of the High Court knows many things which you may not know. You have absolutely no right to say that your matter should be heard by a particular Judge.

Some people imagine that the people of Tamil Nadu may have forgotten these words, steeped in law and justice and worthy of being remembered forever. But the request that one specified judge continue hearing the Bengaluru disproportionate-assets case was rejected. A new judge took up the trial and delivered a ruling yesterday.

This is not the final judgment in the case. When Jayalalithaa's side asked the Supreme Court to allow Judge Balakrishna alone to continue and that request failed, it was felt that the law was performing its duty well. In the same course, Tamil Nadu expects a full and final judgment to arrive at the proper time.
With affection,
M.K.
